indonesian: Piring Tempe Tahu Goreng Renyah (6 potong)

food_subgroup: Fried soy-based protein platter with fermented and non-fermented tofu

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general tempeh food product class (FOODON_03302757). No specific code exists for a combined crispy tempeh-tofu-kremes platter; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Soya bean curd (tofu) and fermented soya products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers soya bean curd and fermented soya products as a category. No exact code for a mixed fried tempeh-tofu platter; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for a mixed fried tempeh-tofu platter dish.

serving.description: 1 platter of 6 pieces (approximately 3 pieces tempeh + 2 pieces tofu + 1 portion kremes)
serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is a platter of 6 pieces totalling approximately 180 g, comprising crispy fried tempeh, deep-fried tofu, and kremes (crispy flour fritters), typically served with gado-gado peanut sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Tempe goreng (fried tempeh) and Tahu goreng (fried tofu) — composite estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a combined crispy tempeh-tofu-kremes platter. Values are estimated from a weighted composite of: (1) USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for tempeh, cooked (FDC ID 174272); (2) USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for tofu, fried (FDC ID 172449); and (3) FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table values for fried tofu and tempeh goreng. Kremes (crispy flour fritters) contribution estimated from USDA fried wheat-flour fritter analogue. Composite weighted approximately 45% tempeh goreng, 35% tahu goreng, 20% kremes by mass. Per-100g values represent the blended platter composition.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of soy-based foods including tempeh and fried tofu — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for tempeh and fried tofu
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a combined crispy tempeh-tofu-kremes platter. GI estimated at 40 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries: tempeh GI approximately 41 (Nuttall et al., peer-reviewed), fried tofu GI approximately 35–42 (soy protein matrix, low GI confirmed in University of Sydney database). Kremes (wheat-flour fritter) component raises GI slightly. Weighted composite GI of 40 is defensible. Glycemic load per serving = (40 × 17.5 g carbohydrate per 100g × 1.8 serving factor) / 100 = approximately 12.6 g per 180 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a full 180 g serving delivers approximately 558 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one or two pieces and avoid additional high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or sambal, as the combined sodium load from the platter and dipping sauces can approach 700–900 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) from soy protein matrix, but moderate carbohydrate from kremes flour fritter component and moderate fat from deep-frying
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI of tempeh and tofu is favourable for glycaemic control, but the kremes component and deep-frying oil increase total energy and carbohydrate load; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a modest portion (2–3 pieces) and pair with non-starchy vegetables rather than rice to manage postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (16 g/100 g) from deep-frying, with relatively low saturated fat (2.8 g/100 g); soy isoflavones in tempeh may confer modest cardioprotective benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The polyunsaturated fat profile from soybean oil is relatively favourable, and tempeh's soy isoflavones may support LDL reduction; however, the deep-frying method and moderate sodium content warrant portion control for individuals with established cardiovascular disease, limiting intake to 2–3 pieces per occasion.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Tempeh Tofu Platter (6 pieces) is an Indonesian fried soy-based protein dish consisting of deep-fried tempeh (fermented soybean cake), deep-fried tofu (tahu goreng), and kremes — light, crispy wheat-flour fritters that add textural contrast. Tempeh is a traditional Indonesian fermented food made by culturing whole soybeans with Rhizopus mould, producing a firm, nutty-flavoured cake with a dense, chewy interior and crispy fried exterior. Tofu pieces are typically firm or medium-firm, fried until golden and slightly puffed. Kremes are thin, lacy, crispy flour fritters that are a hallmark of Indonesian fried chicken and tempeh platters. The platter is commonly served with gado-gado peanut sauce, sambal, or kecap manis. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy, high-protein, moderate-fat food providing approximately 270 kcal, 14.5 g protein, and 16 g fat per 100 g, with a low glycemic index (~40) attributable to the soy protein matrix. Tempeh is a notable source of dietary fibre (3.2 g/100 g), calcium (120 mg/100 g), iron (2.4 mg/100 g), and B vitamins. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and halal. Clinically, the moderate sodium content (~310 mg/100 g) is relevant for hypertension management, and the low GI is favourable for Type 2 Diabetes, though portion control is advised due to the caloric density from deep-frying. Soy isoflavones in tempeh may offer modest cardiovascular benefit.
common_user_queries: Calories in fried tempeh and tofu
Is tempeh tofu platter good for diabetics?
What are kremes in Indonesian food?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific combined platter. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for tempeh (cooked/fried) and tofu (fried), supplemented by FAO/INFOODS SEA values. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for tempeh and fried tofu. Confidence is medium because the composite estimation introduces uncertainty, particularly for the kremes component.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are a weighted composite estimate (45% tempeh goreng, 35% tahu goreng, 20% kremes) derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC IDs 174272 and 172449) and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table. No exact approved-source entry for this combined platter exists. 2. Serving size of 180 g is estimated for a 6-piece platter based on typical Indonesian restaurant portion weights (tempeh ~25 g/piece × 3, tofu ~25 g/piece × 2, kremes ~15 g). 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: tempeh and tofu are plant-based soy products containing no dietary cholesterol; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for tempeh and fried tofu. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: tempeh and tofu are plant-based foods; vitamin B12 is not naturally present in plant foods; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for tempeh or fried tofu, and FAO/INFOODS SEA does not report vitamin D for these items. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for tempeh goreng or tahu goreng, and the USDA SR Legacy entry for tempeh does not include selenium. 7. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for tempeh (~41) and fried tofu (~35–42); the kremes component slightly elevates the composite GI. 8. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (40 × 17.5 / 100) × 1.8 = 12.6 g per 180 g serving. 9. The food is classified as processed due to deep-frying and fermentation processing of tempeh. 10. Kremes are a traditional Indonesian crispy flour fritter garnish; gluten allergen applies due to wheat flour in kremes.
